attributes.stem:
A 50-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department by police after being found wandering in traffic. He appears paranoid and states he does not feel safe. He believes people are trying to kill him. He reports hearing voices warning him about the danger. He is disheveled and malodorous.

He is agitated and pacing within the room. His speech is slurred but comprehensible. There is confusion and disorientation to time. His gait is steady and eye movements are normal. He has difficulty engaging in the assessment due to his preoccupation with his fears. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He drinks alcohol to excess weekly but denies other substance use.

Initial investigations including full blood count, electrolytes, liver function tests, thyroid function tests, and blood alcohol level are within normal limits. Collateral from his next of kin reveals that due to his alcohol use they do not see him very often, however the patient has been calling him expressing these same beliefs over the last 3 weeks.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with acute psychotic symptoms including paranoid delusions and auditory hallucinations in the context of chronic alcohol use. The duration of symptoms and normal blood tests help narrow the differential diagnosis.

Brief psychotic episode is the most likely diagnosis given the acute presentation with psychotic symptoms lasting three weeks. While this is approaching the one-month duration limit for this diagnosis, the sudden onset and lack of previous psychiatric history make this the most likely diagnosis.

Schizophrenia is less likely given the acute onset and relatively short duration. A diagnosis of schizophrenia typically requires at least six months of symptoms including prodromal or residual symptoms, with active symptoms present for at least one month.

Wernicke's encephalopathy typically presents with confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia in the context of thiamine deficiency. While this patient has confusion and alcohol use, the normal gait and eye movements, along with prominent psychotic symptoms, make this diagnosis less likely.

Alcoholic Hallucinosis typically presents with auditory hallucinations in clear consciousness, often during or shortly after heavy alcohol use. While this patient has hallucinations, the presence of confusion and paranoid delusions, along with normal blood alcohol level, make this diagnosis less likely.

Paranoid personality disorder is characterized by long-standing patterns of suspiciousness and mistrust without frank psychotic symptoms. The acute onset of clear delusions and hallucinations rules out this diagnosis.

The management would include ensuring safety, possible antipsychotic medication, investigation of underlying causes including detailed substance use history, and arrangement of psychiatric follow-up. Alcohol cessation support would also be important given the history of excess use.
learningPoints: Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by sudden onset of psychotic symptoms lasting less than one month with full return to premorbid functioning.
Significant psychosocial stressors often precede brief psychotic episodes.
Medical causes including substance use must be excluded before making a psychiatric diagnosis.
references: Stephen A, Lui F. Brief psychotic disorder. StatPearls [internet]. 2021; available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539912/.
Regier DA, Kuhl EA, Kupfer DJ. The DSM-5: Classification and criteria changes. World Psychiatry. 2013;12(2) :92-8.
